title: Game Changer! CBDT Ends TDS on Ship Lease Rentals to IFSC Units under Income Tax Act, 2025
content:
# Game Changer! CBDT Ends TDS on Ship Lease Rentals to IFSC Units under Income Tax Act, 2025

Namaste, my esteemed colleagues! We've just received a very crucial update that's set to significantly streamline the tax landscape for ship leasing activities within our International Financial Services Centres (IFSCs). The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has, through **Notification No. 75/2026, dated July 03, 2026**, ushered in a welcome change, exempting the deduction of tax at source (TDS) on ship lease rentals paid to eligible IFSC units. This is truly a landmark move under the new **Income-tax Act, 2025**, and it's something we all need to understand thoroughly.

Many of us have been grappling with the complexities surrounding TDS on these payments, often leading to cash flow blockages and increased compliance burdens. Well, *ab yeh rule applicable nahi hai* for eligible transactions! Let's dive into the specifics and understand the 'what', 'why', and 'how' of this pivotal notification.

## The 'What': TDS Exemption for IFSC Ship Leasing

Simply put, the CBDT has specified that **no tax shall be deducted at source** on payments categorised as **lease rent** or **supplemental lease rent** made by a lessee to an eligible unit located in an International Financial Services Centre (IFSC) that is engaged in the business of leasing ships. This exemption is a direct result of powers conferred by **Section 400(1) read with Section 147 of the Income-tax Act, 2025**. Prior to this, such payments would typically attract TDS under Section 393(1) [Table S. No. 2] of the said Act.

It's important to note that this isn't just a prospective relief; the notification is **deemed to have come into effect from April 1, 2026**. This means the benefits are available for the current tax framework under the Income-tax Act, 2025, providing a retrospective operational effect right from the beginning of the financial year. And a good thing, *na*, that no person is adversely affected by this retrospective application!

### The New Legislative Framework: Income-tax Act, 2025 & Rules, 2026

Before we proceed, a quick word on the legislative backdrop. For those wondering about the "Income-tax Act, 2025" and "Income-tax Rules, 2026," yes, these are indeed the new frameworks. The Parliament passed the new Income Tax Bill on August 12, 2025, replacing the six-decade-old Income Tax Act, 1961. This new Act simplifies the language and reduces the number of sections significantly. Subsequently, the **Income-tax Rules, 2026**, were officially notified on March 20, 2026, to operationalise this landmark Act, coming into effect from April 1, 2026. So, all our compliances and analyses going forward will be governed by this new regime.

## The 'Why': Boosting India's Maritime Ambitions

This move by the CBDT is a clear signal of the government's commitment to nurturing India's International Financial Services Centres, particularly in the realm of maritime financing and ship leasing. What's the strategic thought behind this?

*   **Enhancing Cash Flow:** By removing the TDS burden, IFSC-based ship leasing entities will experience improved cash flow efficiency. *Seedha seedha fayda toh liquidity ka hai!*
*   **Reducing Compliance Hassles:** Lessees no longer need to deduct tax at source and then reconcile it. This significantly reduces the administrative and compliance burden for businesses involved in ship leasing.
*   **Attracting Investment:** This exemption makes IFSCs a more attractive hub for global ship leasing activities, fostering competition with established international leasing centres.
*   **Strengthening Maritime Ecosystem:** It aligns with India's broader vision to develop a robust maritime financing ecosystem and establish IFSCs as global hubs for such specialised services.

Interestingly, this notification runs in parallel with **Notification No. 74/2026**, also dated July 03, 2026, which grants similar TDS exemption for aircraft lease rentals paid to eligible IFSC units. This dual approach underscores a comprehensive strategy to boost asset leasing from Indian IFSCs.

## The 'How': Conditions for Availing Exemption

While the exemption is a huge relief, it's not unconditional. Both the lessor (the IFSC unit) and the lessee have specific obligations to fulfil:

### Obligations of the IFSC Unit (Lessor):

The cornerstone of availing this exemption lies with the lessor. The IFSC unit must:
*   Furnish a **Statement-cum-Declaration in Form No. 1(N)** to the lessee. This isn't just a formality; it's a critical document.
*   The declaration must clearly specify the **twenty consecutive tax years** for which the lessor opts to claim the deduction available under **Section 147 of the Income-tax Act, 2025**. *Yeh period define karna bahut zaroori hai*.
*   This statement-cum-declaration needs to be furnished and verified in the prescribed manner for *every relevant tax year* covered by the chosen twenty-year deduction period.

### Obligations of the Lessee:

The lessee, upon receiving this declaration from the lessor, has a simplified, yet important, role:
*   The lessee **shall not deduct TDS** on lease rent or supplemental lease rent payments made or credited to the eligible IFSC unit thereafter.
*   However, the lessee is **still required to disclose details** of all such payments on which tax has not been deducted in their prescribed TDS reporting statement under the **Income-tax Rules, 2026**. So, while TDS deduction is out, reporting requirements are still very much *in*.

It is paramount to remember that this TDS exemption is only applicable *during* the twenty consecutive tax years as declared by the lessor. If, for any reason, lease rent is paid outside this declared period, the lessee will remain liable to deduct tax at source as per the applicable provisions.

## Conclusion: A Clear Course for IFSC Ship Leasing

This CBDT notification is a progressive and thoughtful measure that removes a significant tax hurdle for the burgeoning ship leasing sector within India's IFSCs. It reflects a dynamic approach to policy-making, responsive to industry needs and aligned with global best practices. For us professionals, understanding the nuances of this notification, particularly the procedural compliances involving Form 1(N) and the twenty-year window, is key.

This exemption will undoubtedly provide a much-needed impetus, improving liquidity for IFSC units and simplifying compliance for their clients. It sets a clear course for India to emerge as a formidable player in the global ship leasing and maritime financing arena.

*Published on: 5 July 2026*
